studyContent.title: How Urban Local Bodies Work
studyContent.intro: Urban local bodies provide city services and raise money through taxes, fees and government grants.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Urban local bodies do a great deal of work to keep a town or city running. They provide and maintain services such as water supply, drainage and sewage, garbage collection and disposal, street lighting, roads, parks, public health and the control of diseases. To pay for all this, they raise money from taxes such as property tax, from fees and charges for services, and from grants given by the state and central governments. The elected councillors and the appointed officials work together so that the city is clean, safe and well run for all its residents.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Urban local bodies provide city services like water, drains, garbage collection, roads and street lights, paying for them with taxes, fees and government grants.
stepByStep: Provide city services: Urban bodies provide water, drainage, garbage collection, roads, lighting and public health.
Keep the city running: They maintain parks, control diseases and keep the city clean and safe.
Raise money: They collect property tax, fees for services, and grants from higher governments.
Work together: Elected councillors and appointed officials work together to run the city well.

studyContent.title: Work and Its Value
studyContent.intro: Work is any activity done with a purpose, and all honest work has value and dignity.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Work is any activity that people do with a purpose, usually to produce goods or provide services, or to look after themselves and others. Every kind of honest work is valuable, whether it is done by a farmer, a teacher, a doctor, a cleaner or a homemaker. Work meets our needs, keeps society running, and gives people a sense of pride and self-respect. The idea of the dignity of work means that no honest work is high or low; all work deserves respect. Understanding the value of work helps us respect every worker and the contribution they make.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Work is any purposeful activity. All honest work, big or small, has value and deserves respect. This is called the dignity of work.
stepByStep: What work is: Work is any purposeful activity done to make goods, give services or care for others.
All work is valuable: Every kind of honest work, from farming to teaching to cleaning, has value.
Work helps society: Work meets our needs and keeps society running, giving people pride and self-respect.
Dignity of work: The dignity of work means no honest work is high or low; all work deserves respect.

keywords.meaning: The idea that all honest work deserves respect.
keywords.example: Dignity of work means respecting every worker.

keywords.meaning: Pride and respect for oneself.
keywords.example: Honest work gives a person self-respect.

studyContent.title: Economic and Non-Economic Activities
studyContent.intro: Economic activities are done to earn money or a living, while non-economic activities are done without the aim of earning money.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: The work people do can be divided into two kinds. Economic activities are those done to earn money or make a living, such as a shopkeeper selling goods, a farmer selling crops or a doctor treating patients for a fee. Non-economic activities are done not to earn money but out of love, duty or social purpose, such as a mother caring for her child, helping a neighbour, or doing voluntary social service. Both types of work are important. Even though non-economic activities do not earn money, they are very valuable for the family and society.

Non-economic activities: These are done without earning money, out of love, duty or social purpose.
Both are important: Even though non-economic activities earn no money, they are very valuable for family and society.

studyContent.title: The Importance of Non-Economic Activities
studyContent.intro: Non-economic activities, though unpaid, are essential because they support families, communities and society.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Non-economic activities do not earn money, but they are essential to our lives. Much of the work done at home, such as cooking, cleaning, caring for children and looking after elders, is non-economic, yet without it families could not function. Voluntary work in the community, like helping the needy, keeping public places clean or teaching for free, also makes society better. Such work is often not counted in money terms and may go unnoticed, but it has great social value. Recognising the importance of non-economic activities helps us value all those who do this unpaid but vital work.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Non-economic activities are unpaid, like housework and helping others, but they are essential. Families and society could not run without them.

Helping the community: Voluntary work like helping the needy or cleaning public places improves society.
Great social value: Such work is often unnoticed and uncounted in money, but has great social value.

studyContent.title: The Classification of Economic Activities
studyContent.intro: Economic activities are classified into three types: primary, secondary and tertiary activities.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: All the economic activities that produce goods and services can be grouped into three main types. Primary activities use natural resources directly from the Earth, such as farming, fishing, mining and forestry. Secondary activities take the products of primary activities and turn them into new goods, such as making cloth from cotton or furniture from wood; this is also called manufacturing. Tertiary activities provide services rather than goods, such as transport, banking, teaching and trade. These three types depend on one another and together make up the economy. Understanding this classification helps us see how goods and services are produced.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Economic activities are of three types: primary (taking from nature, like farming), secondary (making goods, like factories), and tertiary (services, like transport).
stepByStep: Three types: Economic activities are grouped into primary, secondary and tertiary types.
Primary: Primary activities use natural resources directly, like farming, fishing and mining.
Secondary: Secondary activities turn raw materials into new goods, like making cloth from cotton.
Tertiary: Tertiary activities provide services, like transport, banking and teaching.

keywords.example: Teaching is a tertiary activity.

studyContent.title: Primary Activities
studyContent.intro: Primary activities produce goods directly from nature, such as agriculture, fishing, mining, forestry and animal rearing.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Primary activities are those in which people obtain goods directly from nature. The most important primary activity is agriculture (farming), which gives us food grains, vegetables, fruits and raw materials like cotton. Other primary activities include fishing, rearing animals (like cows, goats and poultry), mining minerals from the Earth, and collecting forest products. A large number of people in India depend on primary activities, especially farming, for their living. Primary activities form the base of the economy because they provide food and the raw materials needed by other activities.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Primary activities take goods straight from nature, like farming, fishing, mining and rearing animals. Many Indians, especially farmers, depend on them.
stepByStep: Goods from nature: Primary activities obtain goods directly from nature.

Other primary activities: These include fishing, rearing animals, mining minerals and collecting forest products.
Base of the economy: Many Indians depend on primary activities, which provide food and raw materials for other work.

studyContent.title: Secondary Activities
studyContent.intro: Secondary activities turn raw materials from primary activities into finished goods, which is called manufacturing.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Secondary activities take the raw materials produced by primary activities and change them into more useful, finished goods. This process is called manufacturing. For example, cotton from farms is turned into cloth and clothing in textile factories, sugarcane is made into sugar, iron ore is made into steel, and wood is made into furniture. Secondary activities add value to raw materials, so the finished goods are worth more than the raw materials. These activities are carried out in workshops, small industries and large factories, and they provide jobs to many people.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Secondary activities make finished goods from raw materials, like turning cotton into cloth. This is manufacturing, done in factories and workshops.
stepByStep: Use raw materials: Secondary activities take raw materials from primary activities.
Make finished goods: They turn these raw materials into useful finished goods, called manufacturing.
Add value: Finished goods are worth more than the raw materials, so value is added.
Provide jobs: These activities happen in workshops and factories and give jobs to many people.

studyContent.title: Tertiary Activities
studyContent.intro: Tertiary activities provide services that support primary and secondary activities, such as transport, trade, banking and education.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: Tertiary activities do not produce goods but provide services that help people and support the other activities. They include transport (moving goods and people), trade (buying and selling, including retail), banking and finance, communication, education, health care and tourism. For example, lorries and trains carry goods from factories to markets, shops sell goods to customers, and banks keep money safe and give loans. Tertiary activities are growing fast and are very important in a modern economy, providing jobs to a large and increasing number of people.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Tertiary activities provide services, not goods, like transport, shops, banks, schools and hospitals. They support other activities and are growing fast.

studyContent.title: A Bottle of Dew (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A folk tale about a lazy man who learns that there is no shortcut to success and that hard work is the real magic.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a folk tale about a man named Rama Natha who believed there was a magic potion that would make him rich without any effort. He goes to a wise sage for help. The clever sage tells him that to make the potion he must collect five litres of dew from banana leaves, but he must plant the banana trees and collect the dew with his own hands. Rama Natha plants rows of banana plants, tends them carefully with his wife Madhumati's help, sells the bananas, and after six years of hard work he becomes prosperous and finally collects the five litres of dew. The sage then reveals that the real 'magic' was Rama Natha's own hard work. The story teaches that there is no shortcut to success.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A lazy man wanted a magic potion to get rich. A wise sage made him grow banana trees and collect dew for years. The hard work itself made him rich, showing there is no shortcut to success.
stepByStep: Beginning: Rama Natha believes in a magic potion that will make him rich without effort and asks a sage for help.
The sage's clever trick: The sage tells him to collect five litres of dew from banana plants he must grow and tend himself.
Years of hard work: Rama Natha plants and tends banana trees with his wife's help and sells the crop, slowly growing prosperous.
The lesson revealed: After six years he succeeds, and the sage reveals the real magic was his own hard work.

studyContent.title: The Raven and the Fox (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A fable-poem by Jean de La Fontaine warning that we should not be fooled by flattery and sweet words.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a fable in the form of a poem, retold from Jean de La Fontaine. A raven sits on a tree holding a piece of food (a morsel) in his beak. A cunning fox, named Reynard, wants the food. The fox flatters the raven, praising his beautiful feathers and saying he would be called the king of birds if only he could sing. The proud raven, forgetting that his voice is only a croak, opens his beak to sing, and the food drops down. The fox quickly grabs it and laughs, teaching the raven a lesson: ignore sweet words that flatter you, because pride is unwise. The poem warns us against being fooled by flattery.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A clever fox flatters a raven holding food, praising his voice. The proud raven opens his beak to sing, drops the food, and the fox grabs it. Lesson: don't be fooled by flattery.
stepByStep: The raven with food: A raven sits on a tree holding a tasty morsel in his beak.
The fox flatters him: The cunning fox praises the raven's feathers and asks him to sing, calling him king of birds.
The raven is fooled: Forgetting his croaky voice, the proud raven opens his beak to sing and drops the food.
The fox's lesson: The fox grabs the food and warns that pride is unwise and one should ignore flattering words.

studyContent.title: Rama to the Rescue (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A clever folk tale in which quick thinking helps a family outwit a thief who has entered their home.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a folk tale about how quick thinking and presence of mind can solve a difficult problem. The word 'rescue' means to save someone from danger. In the story, a thief enters a family's house at night. Instead of panicking, the husband cleverly talks loudly to his wife so the thief overhears, and he uses his cleverness (and the help of the kotwal, the policeman) to trap and catch the thief. The story shows that staying calm and thinking quickly is far better than being frightened and giving up. It encourages us to face problems bravely and find smart solutions.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A thief sneaks into a family's house. Instead of panicking, the clever husband tricks the thief and, with the kotwal's help, gets him caught. It shows that quick thinking solves problems.

Stay calm, think fast: Instead of panicking, the husband thinks quickly about how to handle the thief.
A clever plan: He cleverly talks and acts to trap the thief, using the help of the kotwal (policeman).
The lesson: The thief is caught, showing that quick thinking and courage beat fear and giving up.

studyContent.title: The Unlikely Best Friends (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A story about a lonely royal elephant and a stray dog who become the best of friends, showing friendship can cross all differences.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a heart-warming story about friendship between two very different animals. Gajaraj, a royal elephant, lives in the best stable and has every comfort, but he is sad because he has no friends; only the kind mahout (elephant trainer) looks after him. One evening a tired, hungry stray dog wanders into the stable. Gajaraj kindly shares his food, and the two animals slowly become close, playful friends. When the dog is one day taken away, Gajaraj becomes very sad and stops eating, until the dog is brought back and their friendship is restored. The story shows that true friendship can grow between the most unlikely companions and that friends are very important for happiness.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A lonely royal elephant, Gajaraj, befriends a stray dog who wanders into his stable. They become best friends, showing that friendship can grow between very different creatures.
stepByStep: A lonely elephant: Gajaraj the royal elephant has every comfort but is sad because he has no friend.
A stray dog arrives: A tired, hungry stray dog wanders into the stable, and Gajaraj kindly shares his food.
A close friendship: The elephant and the dog become playful best friends and are happy together.
Friendship restored: When the dog is taken away, the sad Gajaraj stops eating until his friend is returned.

studyContent.title: A Friend's Prayer (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A poem by Jill Wolf in which the speaker prays to be a true, loving friend who accepts friends as they are.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a gentle poem by Jill Wolf about what it means to be a good friend. In the poem, the speaker prays that friendships will always be the most important thing to them, and that they will give their very best to their friends. The speaker wishes to do more than just share hopes and plans; they want to help make their friends' wishes come true. Most importantly, the speaker prays to use the heart to understand friends, to make no judgements 'from afar', and to love friends just the way they are. The poem teaches that true friendship means giving, understanding and accepting friends without judging them.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: In this poem, the speaker prays to be a true friend: to give their best, help friends' wishes come true, and love friends just as they are, without judging them.
stepByStep: Friendship comes first: The speaker prays that friendships will always be the most important thing to them.
Giving your best: The speaker promises to give their very best and do all that a friend can do.
Helping friends: The speaker wishes to help make their friends' hopes and wishes come true.
Accepting friends: The speaker prays to understand friends with the heart and love them just the way they are.

studyContent.title: The Chair (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A story about a show-off boy named Mario who learns the difference between many acquaintances and a few true friends.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a story about a boy named Mario who loved to have lots of friends and showed off a great deal, always boasting about how much he had. Through the events of the story, Mario comes to understand that having many people around who are impressed by his showing off is not the same as having true friends. He learns that real friends are those who care about you for who you are, not for what you have. The story, along with its activities on words for 'friend' (like partner, buddies, companion, classmates), helps us think about the true meaning of friendship and the value of genuine friends over mere show.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Mario loved showing off and having lots of friends. He learns that boasting does not win true friends; real friends care about who you are, not what you have.
stepByStep: Mario shows off: Mario loves having many friends and shows off, boasting about all he has.
Lots of admirers: Many people are around him, impressed by his showing off.
A realisation: Mario learns that being admired for showing off is not the same as having true friends.
True friendship: He understands that real friends care about who you are, not what you own.

studyContent.title: Neem Baba (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A poem in the form of a conversation between a girl, Amber, and a neem tree, celebrating the neem's many medicinal and useful qualities.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a poem written as a friendly conversation between a girl named Amber and a neem tree she lovingly calls 'Neem Baba'. Sitting in the shade of the neem tree in her courtyard, Amber feels the tree is whispering to her and begins to talk with it. Through their conversation, the neem tree tells Amber about its many uses: how its leaves, bark and other parts are used to make medicines, how it keeps people healthy, and how it has served people for a very long time. The poem teaches us to value and respect trees, especially the neem, and reminds us how important nature is to our health and daily life.

The neem's uses: The tree describes how its leaves and bark are used to make medicines and keep people healthy.
Respecting nature: The poem reminds us to value and respect trees like the neem for all they give us.

studyContent.title: What a Bird Thought (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A poem that describes the world from a baby bird's point of view as it grows from its egg and nest and learns to fly out into the wide world.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is an imaginative poem that shows the world through the eyes of a baby bird. At first, the little bird lives in a tiny 'house' (its egg or nest) and thinks that small world is all there is, and it is quite happy there. As it grows, the bird begins to look out and sees a much bigger and more beautiful world outside. Finally, the bird learns to fly and goes out to explore the wide world beyond its nest. The poem gently shows how living things grow, become curious and brave, and discover that the world is much larger than they first thought. It celebrates growth, curiosity and the joy of exploring.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A baby bird first thinks its tiny nest is the whole world. As it grows, it sees a bigger world outside and finally flies out to explore it. The poem celebrates growth and curiosity.
stepByStep: A tiny world: At first the baby bird lives in its little 'house' and thinks that small world is all there is.
Looking out: As it grows, the bird begins to notice a much bigger world outside its nest.
Learning to fly: The bird grows braver and learns to fly out from its nest.
Exploring the world: Finally it goes out to explore the wide, beautiful world beyond, full of curiosity.

studyContent.title: Spices that Heal Us (Prose)
studyContent.intro: An informative letter explaining how common Indian spices are not only used in cooking but also work as natural home remedies.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This lesson is written as a friendly letter that explains how common Indian spices are used to heal us, not just to add taste and colour to food. The writer is happy that some natural cures shared earlier helped the readers (Vikram and Vaibhavi) get better. The letter describes many everyday spices, such as turmeric, fenugreek, cumin, ginger, black pepper, cinnamon, clove and cardamom, and explains how they can be used as simple home remedies for common problems. The writer learnt these cures as a child and encourages the readers to remember them and share them with friends. The lesson highlights the value of traditional Indian knowledge about spices and health.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Written as a letter, this lesson explains how common Indian spices like turmeric, ginger and cumin are used as natural home remedies, not just for cooking. It values traditional knowledge.

Spices as medicine: The letter explains that many spices can also be used as natural cures.

Passing on knowledge: The writer learnt these cures as a child and encourages readers to remember and share them.

studyContent.title: Change of Heart (Prose)
studyContent.intro: A story about a boy named Prabhat who always wanted to win and could not bear losing, until he learns the value of sporting spirit.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a story about a boy named Prabhat who always wanted to win and simply could not bear to lose, not even at a game of marbles. Winning made him feel so good that he never wanted to give up that feeling, and losing upset him greatly. His parents, teachers and friends felt that he did not know how to accept defeat. Through the events of the story, Prabhat has a 'change of heart': he learns that games are meant to be enjoyed, that losing is a part of playing, and that true sporting spirit means being fair, respecting other players and accepting both winning and losing gracefully. The story teaches the importance of good sportsmanship.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Prabhat always wanted to win and hated losing, even at marbles. Through the story he has a change of heart and learns sporting spirit: to play fairly and accept defeat gracefully.

Sporting spirit: He learns that good sportsmanship means being fair and accepting both victory and defeat gracefully.

studyContent.title: The Winner (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A playful poem by Georgia Heard about children enjoying a game outdoors until darkness falls and 'Night wins'.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a lively, playful poem by Georgia Heard about the simple joy of playing outdoors. The poem describes children playing ball in the evening next to a creek in their neighbour's field. They run so fast that they can hardly catch their breath. As evening turns to night, the 'blue dark' turns to black, the cold grass aches their feet, and the trees seem to 'creep close' in the dark. Finally, when it becomes too dark to play, the game is over, and the poet cleverly says 'Night wins!'. The real winner of the game is not a player but the night itself. The poem captures the fun of outdoor play and the beauty of evening turning into night.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Children play ball outdoors in the evening until it gets dark. When night falls and they have to stop, the poet says 'Night wins!' The poem captures the joy of outdoor play.
stepByStep: Evening play: Children play ball in the evening next to a creek in a neighbour's field.
Running fast: They run so fast that they can hardly catch their breath, enjoying the game.
Darkness falls: Evening turns to night: the blue dark turns black, the cold grass aches their feet, and trees seem to creep close.
Night wins: When it is too dark to play, the game ends and the poet says the real winner is the night.

studyContent.title: Yoga - A Way of Life (Prose)
studyContent.intro: An informative lesson explaining what yoga is, its ancient Indian origin, and its benefits for the body, mind and spirit.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is an informative lesson about yoga as a way of life. It explains that yoga is a physical, mental and spiritual practice that began in ancient India. The word 'Yoga' comes from the Sanskrit root 'yuj', which means 'to join' or 'to unite', symbolising the unity of mind and body, thought and action, and harmony between human beings and nature. The lesson notes that the sage Patanjali presented the ancient tradition of yoga in a systematic way in the 'Yoga Sutras'. It describes yoga as a holistic approach to health and well-being. The lesson also reminds us that 21st June is celebrated as International Day of Yoga, highlighting yoga as a proud part of India's heritage and a path to wellness.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Yoga is an ancient Indian practice for body, mind and spirit. The word means 'to unite'. Sage Patanjali wrote the Yoga Sutras, and 21st June is International Yoga Day.
stepByStep: What yoga is: Yoga is a physical, mental and spiritual practice that began in ancient India.
Meaning of the word: 'Yoga' comes from the Sanskrit 'yuj', meaning 'to join' or 'to unite' mind and body.
Patanjali's Yoga Sutras: The sage Patanjali presented yoga systematically in the 'Yoga Sutras'.
Yoga for wellness: Yoga is a holistic path to health, and 21st June is celebrated as International Day of Yoga.

studyContent.title: Hamara Bharat - Incredible India! (Prose)
studyContent.intro: An informative passage celebrating India (Bharat) as a land of wise and heroic people, rich culture, and great natural beauty.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This lesson is a proud celebration of our country, Bharat. It explains that Bharat has been known worldwide as a land of wise and heroic individuals, and that its rich and diverse culture has attracted many travellers since ancient times. The people of Bharat live together in unison, presenting to the world the idea of 'unity in diversity'. The passage describes Bharat as a land rich with rivers, lakes and high mountains, with green forests that are home to many plants, insects, birds and animals. It ends with the advice that, as inhabitants of this great land, we must keep working hard to make sure that Bharat prospers and is respected globally. The lesson fills us with love and pride for our country.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: This lesson celebrates Bharat (India) as a land of wise, heroic people with rich culture, rivers, mountains and forests. It teaches unity in diversity and pride in our country.
stepByStep: Land of wise and heroic people: Bharat has been known worldwide as a land of wise and heroic individuals.
Rich, diverse culture: Its rich and diverse culture has attracted travellers since ancient times, showing unity in diversity.
Natural beauty: Bharat is rich with rivers, lakes, high mountains and green forests full of wildlife.
Our duty: As inhabitants, we must work hard so that Bharat prospers and is respected globally.

studyContent.title: The Kites (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A poem by Daphne Lister in which a child watches colourful kites and wishes to fly high in the sky like them.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is an imaginative poem by Daphne Lister about a child who watches colourful kites flying in the sky. The poet compares the kites to coloured birds in the sky. The child wishes to be small and as light as air, so that they too could ride high up on a kite. The child imagines how much fun it would be to look right down over the park and the rooftops of the town from up in the air, while the people below stand and stare, wishing they could be up there too. The poem beautifully captures a child's imagination, the wish to fly, and the simple joy and freedom of watching kites soar in the sky.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: A child watches colourful kites, which look like coloured birds, and wishes to be light as air to ride high on a kite, looking down over the town. The poem is about imagination and the wish to fly.
stepByStep: Watching the kites: A child watches colourful kites flying high, comparing them to coloured birds in the sky.
Wishing to be light: The child wishes to be small and as light as air so they could ride a kite.
Flying high: The child imagines the fun of looking down over the park and rooftops from high in the air.
People staring below: The child imagines the people below standing, staring and wishing they were up there too.

studyContent.title: Ila Sachani: Embroidering Dreams with her Feet (Prose)
studyContent.intro: An inspiring true story about Ila Sachani, who, born without the use of her hands, became a skilled embroidery artist using her feet.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is an inspiring real-life story about Ila Sachani, who lives in Moti Vavdi village, Bhavnagar, Gujarat. Ila was born without the use of her hands, but she did not let this stop her. With great determination and practice, she learned to do beautiful Kathiawar embroidery using her feet. The lesson invites us to think about how difficult tasks like embroidery, normally done with the hands, can be mastered with the feet through hard work. It also mentions other inspiring people, like Pranav, who achieved great things despite being born without hands. Ila Sachani's story teaches us about courage, determination and never giving up, and shows that disability cannot stop a strong will.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: Ila Sachani from Gujarat was born without the use of her hands but learned to do beautiful Kathiawar embroidery with her feet. Her story shows courage and determination.
stepByStep: Meet Ila Sachani: Ila Sachani lives in Moti Vavdi village, Bhavnagar, Gujarat, and was born without the use of her hands.
Her challenge: Embroidery is usually done with the hands, which made it very hard for Ila.
Her determination: Through great practice and determination, Ila learned to do Kathiawar embroidery with her feet.
An inspiration: Her story, like that of Pranav, inspires us to be brave, determined and never give up.

studyContent.title: National War Memorial (Poem)
studyContent.intro: A patriotic poem honouring the brave soldiers of India remembered at the National War Memorial in New Delhi.
studyContent.ncertBasedExplanation: This is a patriotic poem about the National War Memorial, which was built by the Government of India in honour of our brave soldiers. The poem describes the memorial as a grand and respectful sight in the heart of Bharat, where the names of the soldiers are etched in stone, each name telling a story of courage. The soldiers fought for the country on land, in the skies and on the oceans, standing together side by side for Bharat. The poem speaks of the eternal flame (the Amar Jawan Jyoti) that glows in silence, paying homage to their courage and sacrifice. The poem reminds us to always remember, with pride and respect, the soldiers who gave their lives for the nation.
studyContent.aiSimplifiedExplanation: This patriotic poem honours India's brave soldiers at the National War Memorial in Delhi, where their names are etched in stone and an eternal flame glows in their memory.
stepByStep: Built for soldiers: The National War Memorial was built by the Government of India to honour brave soldiers.
Names etched in stone: Each soldier's name is etched in stone, with each name telling a story of courage.
Sacrifice everywhere: The soldiers fought for Bharat on land, in the skies and on the oceans, side by side.
Eternal flame: The eternal flame (Amar Jawan Jyoti) glows in silence, and the poem asks us to always remember their sacrifice.
